data.title: Judge refuses to release DC pipe bomb suspect
data.description:
A federal judge has rejected a request to be released pending trial from the suspect accused of planting pipe bombs outside the Republican and Democratic national committees ahead of the Jan. 6, 2021, Capitol attack.  

U.S. District Judge Amir Ali, an appointee of former President Biden, ruled Thursday that Brian Cole Jr. must remain locked up while awaiting trial.  

The decision keeps intact a magistrate judge’s ruling that no conditions of release could reasonably keep the public safe from the danger allegedly posed by Cole, who has pleaded not guilty to two federal explosive-related charges.  

Cole’s attorneys pointed to his lack of criminal record and diagnoses for autism and obsessive-compulsive disorder as reasons to let him out of jail as the case progresses. 

They said at a hearing Wednesday that the idea that strict release conditions wouldn’t keep Cole in line is “entirely farcical based on the facts.”  

“The actual nature of the offense matters here,” Ali pushed back. 

Cole’s arrest last month came after what government officials called an “aha moment” led to a breakthrough in a nearly five-year investigation. They’ve said purchasing records, phone data and other evidence were used to identify him. 

In his ruling earlier this month, U.S. Magistrate Judge Matthew Sharbaugh said Cole’s apparent continued purchasing of materials used to make explosives through the summer of 2022 — on top of investigators finding those materials in his home and car upon his arrest — refuted the notion that the past alleged conduct amounted to “some anomalous event.”  

Cole’s attorneys have argued that he had a proclivity for “science experiments,” and that his experimenting with potassium chlorate — a component in explosives — amounted to efforts to create “rocket fuel.” His mother affirmed as much in a sworn declaration submitted to the court that suggested the experiments began earlier than prosecutors have said.  

But Assistant U.S. Attorney Charles Jones said the declaration prompted federal investigators to look further into Cole’s purchasing history, where he said they found that, in 2018, Cole bought beaker sets and the sulfur dust used in the pipe bombs. He said that “fins” and “cones” for a model rocket were not among Cole’s purchases.  

The prosecutor claimed it shows the pipe bomb incident was not isolated, but a “yearslong continuum of conduct.” Defense attorneys rejected the government’s assessment. 

Jones also said that the pipe bombs placed outside the Republican National Committee and Democratic National Committee on Jan. 5, 2021, had “all the components” of a functional explosive device.  

The devices did not detonate five years ago but an explosives examiner for the government was able to explain how the materials in the bombs could be “put…back together” to create a functional device, he said. 

Jones said that Cole told investigators he was “worried” the devices were volatile, allegedly putting them in his backpack one at a time over concern they’d explode on him.  

“He certainly believed that those devices were viable,” the prosecutor said. 

Cole’s next hearing is set for Feb. 27. A trial date has not yet been scheduled.  

If convicted, Cole faces a maximum sentence of 10 years in prison on the count of transporting an explosive device in interstate commerce, while the count of attempted malicious destruction by means of explosive materials carries a five-year minimum sentence and up to 20 years behind bars.

data.title: Queen's Brian May says he's 'thinking twice' about performing in US: 'America is a dangerous place'
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – English musician Brian May, the lead guitar player for Queen, hinted that the band probably would not perform in the U.S. in the near future, if at all.

“America is a dangerous place at the moment, so you have to take that into account,” the 78-year-old rock legend told the Daily Mail.

“It’s very sad because I feel like Queen grew up in America and we love it, but it’s not what it was,” he continued. “Everyone is thinking twice about going there at the moment.”

Brian May performs at the Platinum Jubilee concert taking place in front of Buckingham Palace, London, Saturday June 4, 2022. (AP Photo/Alberto Pezzali, Pool)

The Daily Mail did not say what prompted May’s comments, nor if they were referencing the recent killings of Minneapolis residents Renee Good or Alex Pretti by federal immigration officials deployed to Minnesota. The musician, however, has previously suggested that he was not a fan of President Trump, and had called out Trump’s campaign for using “We Are the Champions” during the 2016 presidential primaries.

“I will make sure we take what steps we can to dissociate ourselves from Donald Trump’s unsavoury campaign,” May remarked on his official website, The Guardian reported at the time.

The guitarist last performed in the U.S. in 2025, appearing with “Beautiful Things” singer Benson Boone during the Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival in Indio, Calif. Queen, with Adam Lambert on lead vocals, had also toured the U.S. in 2019 and 2023.

Speaking with Rolling Stone in 2025, May had also expressed a desire to perform a residency at The Sphere in Las Vegas, telling the outlet that Queen was taking part in “conversations” about it.

I’m very keen on the Sphere,” he told the magazine. “It’s got my mind working.”

May, meanwhile, said he doesn’t know the next time Queen will take the stage at all, let alone in the U.S.

“It’s an unknown,” he told the Daily Mail. “We’ll take it day by day.”

data.title: DOJ opens federal civil rights probe into the death of Alex Pretti, but holds back on Renee Good
data.description:
The Justice Department’s Civil Rights Division will join a variety of federal entities investigating the killing of Minneapolis nurse Alex Pretti, tapping the involvement of a segment of DOJ that reviews excessive force by police.

Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche said the division would work alongside the FBI and a division of the Department of Homeland Security in reviewing the deadly shooting. 

DOJ has faced steep criticism from within the ranks of the department over how it has handled the killings of Pretti and Renee Good, who was also shot by an immigration officer this month.

Blanche, however, cautioned against elevating the role of the Civil Rights Division in the investigation.

“I don’t want the takeaway to be that there’s some massive civil rights investigation that’s happening. This is what I would describe as a standard investigation by the FBI when there’s circumstances like what we saw last Saturday, and that that investigation, to the extent it needs to involve lawyers at the Civil Rights Division, it will involve those,” Blanche said.

“An investigation…means talking to witnesses, it means looking at documentary evidence, sending subpoenas, if you have to, and the Department of Justice, Civil Rights Division has the best experts in the world at this. They’ve been doing it for decades.”

However, Blanche stopped short of saying whether the division would be involved in reviewing the shooting of Good, saying he was “not committing to anything with respect to that investigation.”

“It doesn’t mean that every time that there is a federal-officer related shooting that that’s something Civil Rights takes up. It depends on the circumstances.”

The Washington Post reported Thursday that federal prosecutors in Minneapolis were prepared to resign en masse over how both shootings have been handled. At least half a dozen prosecutors in the office resigned when asked not to probe Good’s death but instead begin investigating her partner.

The two officers that shot Pretti, a VA nurse, have been placed on administrative leave.

Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem said Thursday during an appearance on Fox that while her department is investigating, the FBI had taken the lead.

The bureau is also the lead investigative agency on the Good shooting, and has thus far refused to share any information with Minnesota officials.

“We will continue to follow the investigation that the FBI is leading and giving them all the information that they need to bring that to conclusion, and make sure that the American people know the truth of the situation and how we can go forward and continue to protect the American people,” Noem said while speaking with Sean Hannity.

data.title: Trump says he didn't discuss rate cuts with Warsh: 'Inappropriate'
data.description:
President Trump told reporters in the Oval Office on Friday that he did not discuss interest rate cuts with his Federal Reserve chair pick Kevin Warsh, insisting that would be “inappropriate.” 

When asked whether Warsh had committed to him that he would cut interest rates if he is confirmed, Trump said, “no, but we talk about it.” 

“I’ve been following him. I don’t want to ask him that question. I think it’s inappropriate, probably,” he continued. “It probably would be allowed, but I want to keep it nice and pure. But he certainly wants to cut rates. I’ve been watching him for a long time.”

Trump’s comments came hours after he announced he was picking Warsh, a member of the Federal Board of governors, to be his nominee to chair the board on Friday. 

Warsh will have to be confirmed by the Senate, and is already facing some resistance from Republican Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.). 

In a post on X on Friday, Tillis said Warsh is a “qualified nominee with a deep understanding monetary policy,” but said he will oppose the confirmation of any Federal Reserve nominee until the Justice Department’s criminal investigation into current Chair Jerome Powell is “fully and transparently resolved.” 

Trump addressed Tillis’s remarks when asked on Friday, saying “that kind of thinking is why he is no longer a senator.” 

“He’s going to be out of office,” he said, referring to Tillis’s retirement. “I always liked Sen. Tillis actually, but he did some things that were not smart.”

“If he doesn’t approve we’ll just have to wait until someone comes in that will approve it,” he said, referring to Tillis as wanting to be “an obstructionist.” 

The president also addressed the investigation into Powell, but did not clarify whether he believes it should continue. 

“It’s either gross incompetence or it’s theft,” Trump said, referring to the renovation project happening at the Federal Reserve, which he said he has heard will cost between $3 and $4 billion. “So he’s either incompetence or he or somebody is a crook.”

data.title: Holder on Gabbard presence at Fulton County raid: 'Huge concerns'
data.description:
Former Attorney General Eric Holder (D) said Thursday he has “huge concerns” about Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard’s presence during the recent FBI raid of election offices in Georgia, arguing she has “no role” in domestic activities.

“I have huge concerns. The director of national intelligence heads agencies that look out from our borders into foreign nations,” Holder told CNN anchor Laura Coates. “Look at our enemies, those who do harm to the nation.”

Several Democrats have called for an explanation of why Gabbard was on the scene, after a photograph showed her at the Fulton County, Ga. site where federal officers were executing a search warrant for records related to the 2020 election.

A senior government official defended her in a statement to The Hill’s sister network, NewsNation, saying Gabbard “has a pivotal role in election security and protecting the integrity of our elections against interference, including operations targeting voting systems, databases, and election infrastructure.”

The Georgia county emerged at the center of President Trump’s focus following his loss to former President Biden in the 2020 presidential election. In a phone call months later, Trump pressured Secretary of State Brad Raffensperger (R) to “find 11,780 votes,” roughly the number of ballots needed to flip the state in his favor.  

Fulton County District Attorney Fani Willis (D) indicted Trump in 2023, alleging he and more than a dozen others conspired to subvert the results of the election. Willis was later disqualified from the case after her romantic relationship with the special prosecutor she hired to lead the election interference case was unveiled.

The Justice Department filed a complaint against Fulton County a month later, requesting “all used and void ballots, stubs of all ballots, signature envelopes, and corresponding envelope digital files” from the 2020 election to investigate “compliance with federal law.”

The case was ultimately dismissed following the president’s 2024 election win.

Holder, who served under former President Obama, expressed worries on Friday that the Trump administration’s focus on the 2020 election was aimed at influencing the upcoming midterm elections.

“What I fear most here is that it’s not really an examination of what happened in 2020. It’s laying the basis for perhaps an executive order of some sort to try and have on the 2026 election,” he told the CNN host.

The former attorney general also predicted that Democrats would fare well “if this election is held fairly” despite mid-cycle redistricting attempts in Republican-led states what he called the GOP’s “usual voter suppression.” But he also warned that the FBI’s actions in the Peach State could be a harbinger of future efforts to undermine the election.  

“This is another front that they potentially are going to open up on the basis of this investigation that is being apparently run by a United States Attorney in Missouri, sending the Director of National Intelligence to Georgia to look at an election that was held almost six years ago,” Holder said.

“I mean, go figure this out. It makes no sense, but it’s alarming, and it is extremely dangerous,” he added.

Bloomberg Law reported Thursday that Attorney General Pam Bondi had tapped Thomas Albus, the chief prosecutor in the Eastern District of Missouri, to lead election integrity probes nationwide.

data.title: Live updates: Senate aims for funding package vote; DOJ releases remaining Epstein files
data.description:
The Senate is aiming for a vote Friday afternoon on a funding package that would fund the government as it heads for a partial shutdown at midnight. The chamber had hit a snag in its deal-making when Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) placed a hold on the package.

But he relaxed his stance on Friday morning, allowing for a vote in exchange for a later vote on legislation to the conduct of state and local officials who refuse to cooperate with federal immigration enforcement operations.

On the table is a package of five full-year funding bills and a stopgap measure funding the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) for two weeks.

Earlier Friday, President Trump announced his choice to succeed Jerome Powell as chair of the Federal Reserve, naming Kevin Warsh to the role.

“I have known Kevin for a long period of time, and have no doubt that he will go down as one of the GREAT Fed Chairmen, maybe the best,” Trump said of Warsh, a former Fed governor who was runner-up to Powell for chair in 2017.

The Justice Department on Friday said it had fulfilled its congressional mandate in making public the remaining 3 million documents in the Jeffrey Epstein files.

Follow live here.

data.title: Cotton PAC polling says voters want DHS to 'tone down'
data.description:
Polling from a group linked to Sen. Tom Cotton (R-Ark.) found that a generous sum of Americans want Immigration and Customs Enforcement to “tone down” its tactics, according to a report from Punchbowl News.

A poll by America One Policies PAC showed that 41 percent of respondents don’t want “immigration and enforcement and deportations” to stop but want ICE “to avoid unnecessary harm and confrontation” during detainments — a statement with which 88 percent of Republicans and independents agree. 

Twenty-four percent of voters said they agreed that “ICE’s aggressive tactics are necessary” and that “Illegal immigration is a serious problem and we need tough enforcement to remove people who are here illegally,” Punchbowl reported.

The poll also found that 78 percent of respondents supported deporting immigrants living in the U.S. illegally and those who have criminal records. Fifty-six percent said they want to deport immigrants living in the U.S. illegally and those who arrived within the last four years, while 52 percent reported supporting a mass deportation of all undocumented immigrants in the U.S., according to Punchbowl.

Cotton said in June he didn’t support a “pullback on any kind of enforcement at all” after the Trump administration ordered a pause on ICE raids in the agriculture, hotel and restaurant industries.

“I don’t think we should pull back on any kind of enforcement at all,” Cotton told CBS News’s Margaret Brennan on “Face The Nation” at the time. “I think worksite enforcement in all industries needs to be able to move forward. And I think ICE agents on the front lines need the support of political leadership.”

The Hill has reached out to the PAC and to Cotton’s office. It was unclear when the poll was in the field and how many people were polled.

data.title: Trump signs executive order bringing IndyCar race to streets of DC
data.description:
President Trump signed an executive order on Friday that will bring the IndyCar race to the streets of Washington, D.C. this summer as a part of the country’s 250th anniversary. 

The race’s theme, which will be dubbed ‘Greatness with American Motorracing,’ will take place by the National Mall the weekend of August 21 to 23. 

“It’s really having to do with our great 250th birthday where we’re having so many wonderful things,” Trump said. “This is going to have a lot of people. I won’t even say how many people because I think it could set a record for racing.” 

The president was joined for the signing by Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy, Interior Secretary Doug Burgum, White House staff secretary Will Scharf, IndyCar owner and former professional racecar driver Roger Penske, and Fox Sports CEO Eric Shanks.  

Fox Sports is the television outlet that airs the IndyCar races. 

“This project was kind of on life support until you stepped in recently and got us here today,” Shanks said. “To be able to celebrate America’s birthday on Fox is an absolutely incredible honor.” 

Logistically, it’s unclear how the race will be laid out in the center of the nation’s capitol. However, Penske Corporation President Bud Denker called Washington, D.C. Mayor Muriel Bowser (D) “a great partner” in the process. 

The event is one of many taking place that is being touted by the White House, including the FIFA World Cup set to take place in the U.S. and a UFC fight that is slated to take place in front of the White House in June.

data.title: Republican Senate campaign arm raised $88 million in 2025
data.description:
The National Republican Senatorial Committee (NRSC) raised $88 million in 2025, the campaign arm announced on Friday, as the party fights to hold onto the Senate in the midterms later this year. 

It’s the NRSC’s best off-year fundraising haul since 2021, leaving nearly $20 million in the bank as Republicans enter 2026. 

“We’re raising record amounts of money, building an all-star team, and delivering on Trump’s agenda,” NRSC Chairman Tim Scott said in a statement shared first with The Hill. 

“That off-year foundation will be the difference in winning this November. 2025 was an incredible year, but now is the time to put those resources to work to defend our Senate Majority. We’re just getting started!” he said.

After starting 2025 with around $3 million cash on hand, according to FEC filings, the NRSC said it paid off more than $26 million in debt and bills from its successful campaign to win control of the upper chamber in 2024. 

This year’s haul is roughly $10 million more than what the NRSC saw in off-year 2023, according to a source familiar. 

Republicans are on defense this cycle in the Senate, where they hold a 53-47 majority as Democrats grow bullish about taking control of the chamber this fall.

Other campaign operations have yet to announce their end-of-year totals, but the Democrats’ Senate campaign arm ended November with almost $19 million cash on hand. 

The fundraising arm for House Democrats nearly doubled their cash on hand throughout the year, closing November with $46 million, just ahead of the National Republican Congressional Committee’s (NRCC) $45 million war chest.

The Republican National Committee (RNC) climbed to nearly $90 million cash on hand in November, according to filings — while the Democratic National Committee (DNC) lost money, ending with $12 million in November.

data.title: China, Russia and Iran are investing billions to influence the US midterms
data.description:
On Jan. 15, Lt. Gen. Joshua Rudd, President Trump’s nominee to lead the National Security Agency and U.S. Cyber Command, pledged before the Senate Armed Services Committee to use the full weight of the nation’s cyber capabilities to protect the U.S. from foreign interference in the upcoming midterm elections. 

It was a reassuring signal that the military apparatus remains vigilant. But the reality of modern information warfare is that securing our midterm election requires far more than Fort Meade. It requires a synchronized, whole-of-government effort that spans the interagency — but it ultimately starts with political will at the White House. 

Rudd’s commitment was particularly welcome because China, Russia and Iran have drastically increased their budgets for influence operations. Russia, for instance, has made a calculated trade-off. Its 2026 budget increased funding for state-run media and information operations by 54 percent — an additional $458 million. As Ukraine’s foreign ministry has noted, Moscow has determined that maintaining the “firehose of falsehood” is as critical to its survival as the supply of artillery shells. 

China, likewise, is investing in the information domain at levels that reflect the importance Beijing places on soft power in the age of AI. Based on forensic analysis of China’s 15th Five-Year Plan (for 2026 to 2030) and current fiscal trajectories, Beijing is re-architecting its operations into a capital-intensive “Cognitive Domain” war machine. 

Aggregate annual spending on foreign influence is projected to exceed $10 billion this year. This figure includes the United Front Work Department — the Chinese agency tasked with co-opting foreign elites — whose budget alone, adjusted for current fiscal trajectories, is projected to reach $4 billion. To put that figure in stark perspective: Beijing now spends more on influence operations than the entire operating budget of the Chinese Ministry of Foreign Affairs.  

Even Iran, grappling with hyperinflation exceeding 40 percent, has prioritized this domain. The 2025 budget for the Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting was set at approximately $580 million, a 46 percent increase year-over-year. In a country where the economy is crippled, the regime has explicitly chosen to starve civil infrastructure to feed its propaganda machine. 

The billions flowing into these operations are funding a new kind of weapon: industrialized artificial intelligence. This technology has fundamentally altered the economics of influence. In 2016, interference required expensive “troll farms” — buildings full of human operators manually typing posts. Today, generative AI automates that labor, allowing a single adversary to manage thousands of distinct personas that produce unique, convincing content at infinite scale. 

Simultaneously, the barrier to entry has collapsed. What once required the resources of a state intelligence service now requires little more than a commercially available subscription, granting every malign actor the power to flood America’s social media feeds and news with falsehoods. That is why, crucially, efforts to protect our midterm elections must begin now. 

For too long, Washington has operated under a strategic misconception I call the “Super Bowl Fallacy.” Inside the State Department and across the federal government, elections are treated as the “Super Bowl” of malign influence — a scheduled, high-stakes spectacle that begins a few months before voting starts and ends when the networks call the race. Under this mindset, we scramble resources late in the season to “secure the vote,” standing up task forces and surging intelligence capabilities. 

But now more than ever, the information space is a perpetually shifting mosaic, and the true strength of a democracy is forged or fractured in the “quiet years” between elections. It is in these intermediate periods — the “off-season” — during which our adversaries take advantage. They are deepening social fissures, planting harmful narratives and building the AI-enabled bot networks that will be activated well ahead of November’s elections. There is no “push button” solution that can undo years of adversarial conditioning in a single news cycle. 

Gen. Rudd’s pledge is vital, but it is insufficient. As we navigate the weaponization of artificial intelligence and increased adversary budgets for malign influence, both Congress and the White House must pledge to the American public that they will do everything they can to ensure the sovereignty of our elections. 

If we wait until the weeks before the election to confront these influence operations, we will be fighting for voters who have already been targeted, profiled and manipulated by adversaries who began their work years ago. To delay action now is a costly mistake.  

Darjan Vujica served as a senior official at the State Department and was most recently the emerging technology coordinator at the U.S. Embassy in New Delhi and director of the Global Engagement Center’s Analytics Directorate.
